steps.title: Identify the horizontal asymptote
steps.content: For an exponential function of the form $f(x)=ab^x+c$, the graph has horizontal asymptote $y=c$. In the picture, the curve levels off near $y=-7$ as it goes left. So $c\approx-7$, not $0$.

steps.title: Compare the graph to the x-axis
steps.content: The question asks how many times $f(x)=0$, which means how many times the graph reaches the $x$-axis. The visible curve is already below $y=-7$, and an exponential graph of this form stays entirely on one side of its horizontal asymptote. Since the asymptote is below the $x$-axis, the whole graph stays below the $x$-axis.

steps.title: Confirm with a representative graph
steps.content: A representative graph with the same key behavior is $y=-7-2^x$: it approaches $y=-7$ and never touches $y=0$. The given graph behaves the same way, so there are zero values of $x$ for which $f(x)=0$. Choice D is correct.
